planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped hollies in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Nellie R. Stevens is self-fruitful and does not absolutely require a male pollinizer, though nearby male hollies increase berry production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Nellie R. Stevens is the most widely planted evergreen holly in the American South — a vigorous, broadly pyramidal tree-form shrub reaching 15–25 feet tall with a dense, naturally symmetrical form that requires minimal pruning to maintain its attractive shape. It is a hybrid of Ilex cornuta × Ilex aquifolium, combining the heat tolerance and gloss of Chinese holly with the cold hardiness and berry production of English holly. The dark, glossy, spiny evergreen leaves provide a rich, formal appearance year-round, and the plant produces abundant clusters of bright red berries from fall through winter that are highly attractive to cedar waxwings, mockingbirds, and other wildlife. Nellie R. Stevens is considered partially self-fruitful — it sets fruit without a pollinizer, but nearby male hollies (any male Ilex cornuta or I. aquifolium) significantly increase fruit load. It is adaptable to a wide range of soil types and performs well in Gulf Coast clay soils once established. Established plants are quite drought-tolerant. In zone 9, Nellie R. Stevens is one of the most reliable large evergreen hollies, providing both year-round screening and spectacular winter berry displays.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Savannah is female and requires a nearby male holly (Ilex opaca or I. cassine) within 50 feet for reliable berry production.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Savannah holly is a naturally occurring hybrid between Dahoon holly (Ilex cassine) and American holly (Ilex opaca) — both southeastern native hollies — producing a fast-growing, upright-pyramidal evergreen tree valued across the Gulf South for its rapid establishment, lighter, finer-textured foliage than other landscape hollies, and exceptional production of small, bright-red berries on female plants throughout winter. The relatively light-weight, less spiny leaves give Savannah a more graceful appearance than the coarser-textured hollies, and the gray bark is attractive. It grows faster than American holly and adapts to a wider range of soil conditions, including periodic wetness, that would stress other hollies. A single female Savannah holly laden with red berries in winter is one of the most attractive of all native evergreen trees for wildlife gardening — the berries are a critical winter food source for cedar waxwings, robins, and mockingbirds. A male pollinator nearby is needed for consistent berry production; any compatible male Ilex opaca or I. cassine in the vicinity will serve. Excellent for screening, naturalizing, and as a specimen tree in the Gulf South.
sources: USDA Forest Service — Native Holly Species

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; East Palatka is female and requires a male Ilex opaca or I. cassine within 50 feet for heavy berry production.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; East Palatka holly is a naturally occurring hybrid of Dahoon holly (Ilex cassine) and American holly (Ilex opaca) selected from natural hybrid populations near Palatka, Florida, and has been widely used in southeastern landscape plantings for decades. Like Savannah holly, East Palatka is a large, fast-growing, upright-pyramidal evergreen tree, but it is distinguished by a somewhat more open branching habit and leaves with typically only a single apical spine at the tip rather than the multiple spines of standard American holly, making it less prickly and easier to handle. The single-spined leaves and the plant's natural origin deep in the Gulf Coastal Plain make it exceptionally well-adapted to zone 9 heat and humidity. Female plants loaded with small red berries in fall and winter are spectacular wildlife plants, attracting migrating and wintering songbirds. East Palatka is particularly well-suited to Florida, coastal Alabama, Mississippi, and Louisiana conditions where the high humidity and heat stress some landscape hollies. Requires a male pollinizer for heavy fruit production. Widely available in Gulf Coast nurseries and highly recommended by the University of Florida IFAS Extension for sustainable Gulf Coast landscapes.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Burford is self-fruitful and produces abundant berries without a male pollinizer — a distinct advantage over most other landscape hollies.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Burford holly is one of the most commonly planted landscape hollies in the South — a large, dense, rounded to broadly mounding shrub reaching 8–15 feet tall with exceptionally glossy, dark-green foliage and notably reduced spines compared to the species (most leaves have only a single terminal spine rather than the species' four-five-spined leaves). This reduced spininess makes Burford far easier to work around than typical Chinese hollies. The outstanding feature that separates Burford from all other landscape hollies is its self-fruitfulness: female plants set abundant, large, bright-red berries every year without any male pollinizer plant nearby — a critical advantage in residential settings where only one plant will be grown. The red berries ripen in fall and typically persist through winter. Burford is adaptable to a wide range of soils and extremely heat-tolerant, performing reliably in Gulf Coast zone 9 without the summer stress that affects some hollies. A dwarf form ('Dwarf Burford Holly', Ilex cornuta 'Burfordii Nana') reaches 3–4 feet and is suitable for foundation plantings and low hedges. Prune in late winter or spring after berries have served their wildlife purpose.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Foster No. 2 is the female (berry-producing) form widely sold in commerce; Foster No. 1 is the male pollinizer form and should be planted nearby for best berry production.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Foster holly is a hybrid of American holly (Ilex opaca) and Dahoon holly (Ilex cassine) selected from a population in Birmingham, Alabama, by E.E. Foster in 1954. It is one of the most widely planted landscape hollies in the South and is distinguished by a distinctly narrower, more columnar habit than most other hollies — making it ideal for tight spaces, narrow screens, and situations where a tall, slim evergreen accent is needed. The small, somewhat spiny, dark-green leaves and dense, narrow growth create a refined, formal appearance. Female plants (the widely sold 'Foster No. 2') produce abundant small red berries that ripen in fall and persist through winter, attracting birds. The narrow habit and attractive berries make Foster holly an outstanding street tree, screening plant between properties, and formal landscape accent. Male plants ('Foster No. 1') must be planted nearby for reliable berry production. Foster holly is notably adaptable to the Gulf Coastal Plain's humid, hot summers and performs well in zone 9 where it is widely used in formal residential and commercial landscapes.
